summary: It's been a mixed bag for the SE BeltLine this weekend. The Beacon in Grant Park is struggling with major vacancy issues, while neighbors are gearing up to fight a massive homeless shelter proposed for the Westside BeltLine. Meanwhile, Atlanta United finally got their first win of the season.

topStory.headline: The Beacon Grant Park Struggles as Vendors Flee
topStory.summary: The trendy Grant Park warehouse district that thrived during COVID is now facing a serious vacancy crisis. What used to be a packed destination connected to the BeltLine's Southside Trail now sits mostly empty, with only about 35% of retail spaces occupied. It's a stark reminder of how quickly things can change in Atlanta's development game.
topStory.body:
Remember when The Beacon felt like the coolest place in Atlanta? During the pandemic, this Grant Park warehouse district was absolutely buzzing with artists, food vendors, and that perfect brewery patio vibe that made it feel like one giant hangout spot. The direct connection to the BeltLine's Southside Trail didn't hurt either.

Now? It's a different story entirely. Kirkwood resident Taylor Cross, who used to be a regular, was shocked to see how empty the place has become on a recent visit. He estimates maybe 35% of the retail space is actually occupied these days. That's a dramatic fall from grace for a place that seemed to have figured out the perfect formula just a few years ago.

The timing is particularly rough given all the development pressure along the BeltLine corridor. The Beacon showed how creative, affordable spaces could thrive in Grant Park, but rising costs and changing dynamics seem to have caught up with what was once a model for how to do mixed-use development right.
topStory.imageAlt: The Beacon warehouse district in Grant Park showing vacant retail spaces

It's a stark reminder of how quickly things can change in Atlanta's development game.

stories.headline: Westside Neighbors Organize Against Massive BeltLine Homeless Shelter
stories.summary: The Atlanta Mission wants to build a 900-bed homeless shelter on prime BeltLine real estate where the Stars Metals District was supposed to go. Neighbors are calling it a missed opportunity and organizing under the banner "Westside for Economic Justice" to fight the proposal. They're asking why this valuable land can't be used for something that actually benefits the community long-term.
stories.body:
You know that massive site along the new Westside BeltLine where developers were planning the Stars Metals District? Well, scratch those plans. The Atlanta Mission wants to put a 900-bed homeless shelter there instead, and neighbors are not having it.

A community group called "Westside for Economic Justice" has organized a March 26 meeting to oppose the plan. They're asking Atlanta City Council to reject the Atlanta Mission's request for a Special Use Permit, arguing this prime BeltLine real estate should be used for economic development that actually lifts up the community. It's hard to argue with their logic, honestly.

This feels like a repeat of the same pattern we've seen across Atlanta, where low-income communities get stuck with all the services nobody else wants while missing out on the economic benefits of development. The Westside has been waiting for its BeltLine moment for years, and neighbors understandably don't want to see it turned into a massive institutional use instead of the mixed-use development that could actually create jobs and opportunities.
stories.imageAlt: Proposed site for Atlanta Mission homeless shelter along Westside BeltLine

stories.headline: Atlanta Makes Big World Cup Promises with 95 Days to Go
stories.summary: With just over three months until FIFA World Cup matches hit Mercedes-Benz Stadium, Atlanta officials are making some ambitious promises. MARTA's rolling out their new payment system in two weeks, the airport's finally opening that long-delayed South Terminal, and Centennial Olympic Park is getting transformed into a massive fan festival. The question is whether they can actually deliver.
stories.body:
Atlanta's World Cup organizers gathered at Mercedes-Benz Stadium this week to make their pitch: the city is ready. With 95 days until the tournament, they unveiled some pretty big promises that honestly make me a little nervous given Atlanta's track record with major projects.

The good news? MARTA says they'll roll out their Better Breeze payment system in just two weeks and boost service during matches. The airport is finally, supposedly, opening the South Terminal that's been under construction forever. And Centennial Olympic Park is getting converted into what sounds like the world's biggest tailgate party.

But let's be real here. This is the same city that struggled with snow removal and had airport power outages during major events before. The World Cup will bring hundreds of thousands of visitors to town, and our infrastructure will be tested like never before. I'm cautiously optimistic, but Atlanta definitely has some proving to do.
stories.imageAlt: World Cup planning event at Mercedes-Benz Stadium

stories.headline: Emergency Water Work Shuts Down Key Midtown Intersection for Month
stories.summary: Starting Monday, the city is closing the intersection at 10th Street and Hemphill Avenue for about four weeks to replace a major water valve. It's part of Atlanta's ongoing effort to fix our aging water infrastructure, but the timing couldn't be worse with everything else going on in Midtown right now.
stories.body:
If you drive through Midtown regularly, prepare to find a new route. The city is shutting down the intersection at 10th Street NW and Hemphill Avenue NW starting Monday for what they're calling "emergency" water valve replacement work.

This is all part of Atlanta's water distribution maintenance program, which honestly is long overdue given the state of our pipes. The work will run Monday through Saturday from 7 a.m. to 7 p.m. for approximately four weeks, weather permitting.

The timing is pretty rough though. Midtown is already dealing with construction projects all over the place, and this intersection closure is going to force even more traffic onto already crowded alternate routes. The city says they'll have detour signs up, but we all know how well those usually work. Plan extra time if you're heading through that area.
stories.imageAlt: Construction work on Atlanta street intersection

stories.headline: Mayor Dickens Refuses to Sign Fire Union Contract, Attacks Union Leader
stories.summary: Mayor Andre Dickens ignored Friday's deadline to sign the firefighters' union contract that City Council approved almost a year ago. Instead of explaining the delay, he's questioning union president Nate Bailey's legitimacy and claiming there were "irregularities" in his election. It's a messy situation that makes the mayor look petty while firefighters wait for their first-ever union contract.
stories.body:
This is not a good look for Mayor Dickens. The Atlanta Fire Rescue union gave him a Friday deadline to sign their contract, and instead of just signing the thing, he's gone on the attack against union president Nate Bailey. Keep in mind, City Council unanimously approved this contract almost a year ago.

Dickens is claiming there were "irregularities" in Bailey's re-election as union president last November and says the union will have to redo the election in May. His statement to Atlanta Civic Circle reads like political mudslinging rather than a reasonable explanation for why he won't sign a contract the city has already approved.

Here's the thing: Atlanta firefighters have never had a union contract before, and they've been waiting patiently while the mayor plays political games. This feels like the kind of unnecessary drama that makes people lose faith in city government. Either sign the contract Council approved or give a real reason why you can't. Don't attack the messenger.

stories.headline: Decatur Schools Face Another Year of Budget Shortfalls
stories.summary: The draft budget for City Schools of Decatur is projecting dramatic losses again for fiscal year 2027. A concerned resident wrote to Decaturish expressing disappointment that the district keeps running deficits despite being one of the state's most well-funded school systems. It raises questions about financial management in one of metro Atlanta's most desirable school districts.
stories.body:
City Schools of Decatur has a reputation as one of the best school districts in Georgia, which is exactly why so many families move to the area. But the latest budget draft shows the district projecting major losses again for the fiscal year ending in June 2027.

A reader wrote to Decaturish expressing frustration that keeps happening year after year. And honestly, it's a fair question. Decatur schools benefit from some of the highest property values in the metro area, so where is all that tax revenue going? The district consistently ranks among the best-funded in the state per pupil.

This matters beyond just Decatur because the school district is a huge draw for families looking at where to live ITP. If the finances are shaky, it could affect property values and the whole character of the area. Someone needs to explain why one of Georgia's most well-funded districts keeps running in the red.

stories.headline: Four-Year-Old Killed in Southwest Atlanta Domestic Dispute
stories.summary: Atlanta police found a severely injured 4-year-old during a domestic dispute call Saturday night on Alison Court in southwest Atlanta. Officers had to force entry into the locked apartment and used de-escalation techniques to get the child away from a man who was taken into custody. Tragically, the child died at the hospital from apparent stab wounds.
stories.body:
This is the kind of news that just breaks your heart. Atlanta police responded to what started as a domestic dispute call Saturday night on Alison Court in southwest Atlanta, but it turned into something much worse when they found a severely injured 4-year-old inside a locked apartment.

According to homicide Lt. Christopher Butler, officers had to force entry into the apartment because it was locked. They used de-escalation techniques to get the child away from the man inside and took him into custody. But the child was already badly injured from apparent stab wounds before police arrived.

The 4-year-old was rushed to Grady but didn't survive. It's a reminder of how domestic violence affects the most vulnerable members of our community. This happened in a neighborhood that's been working hard to improve, and incidents like this highlight why community support and intervention services are so crucial.

stories.headline: One Dead, Two Critical in Downtown Athens Triple Shooting
stories.summary: A Saturday morning shooting in downtown Athens left 22-year-old Clayton Adams dead and two others in critical condition. Police arrested someone in connection with the shooting that happened in a parking lot on North Jackson Street around 2:30 a.m. One of the other victims may not survive, according to police.
stories.body:
Downtown Athens saw another violent incident early Saturday morning when gunfire erupted in a parking lot on North Jackson Street around 2:30 a.m. The shooting left three people wounded, with 22-year-old Clayton Adams of Hull not surviving his injuries.

Police have arrested someone in connection with the shooting, though they haven't released the suspect's name yet. The other two victims, both in their early twenties, were rushed to the hospital in critical condition. Officers say one of them is not expected to live.

This happened in the 400 block of North Jackson Street, not far from the University of Georgia campus, though police say none of the victims had connections to the university. It's another reminder that Athens, like many college towns, deals with violence that often has nothing to do with the student population but affects everyone's sense of safety downtown.

stories.headline: Atlanta's Shunika Terry Up for Oscar for 'Sinners' Hair Work
stories.summary: Shunika Terry, an Atlanta hairstylist who never thought she'd work in film, is nominated for an Academy Award for her work as hair department head on 'Sinners.' She's proof that Atlanta's film industry has created opportunities for local talent to reach the highest levels of their craft. The Oscars air Sunday night.
stories.body:
Shunika Terry had it all planned out. The Atlanta hairstylist set up her camera the night before the Oscar nominations were announced, ready to capture the moment whether she got nominated or not. When the technical difficulties hit and her husband's fancy setup failed, she just grabbed her phone and hit record.

Terry is nominated for Best Makeup and Hairstyling for her work as hair department head on "Sinners," the film that received a record 16 Oscar nominations this year. What makes this story even better is that Terry never thought she'd work in film. She's built her career right here in Atlanta, taking advantage of the opportunities that the city's booming film industry has created.

"Sinners" was shot in Georgia, and Terry's nomination is a perfect example of how Atlanta's film boom has elevated local talent to the highest levels of the industry. She's competing against the biggest names in Hollywood, but she got there by mastering her craft right here at home. The Oscars air Sunday night, and all of Atlanta should be rooting for her.
stories.imageAlt: Shunika Terry with Michael B. Jordan at Oscar luncheon

stories.headline: Alliance Theatre Picks Winner of National Playwriting Competition
stories.summary: JuCoby Johnson's "The Red Man" won the 23rd annual Alliance/Kendeda National Graduate Playwriting Competition. The Juilliard student's Southern Gothic thriller will be produced as part of the Alliance's 2027/28 season, while four other finalists get workshop productions next year.
stories.body:
The Alliance Theatre just announced the winner of their prestigious national playwriting competition, and it's going to be a good one. JuCoby Johnson, currently at Juilliard, won with "The Red Man," which they're describing as a Southern Gothic thriller and love story.

Johnson's play will get a full production as part of the Alliance's 2027/28 season, which is a huge deal for any playwright. The competition has become one of the most important launching pads for new theatrical work in the country, and winning it can really change a writer's career trajectory.

Four other finalists will get developmental workshops as part of next year's Alliance/Kendeda Week, including plays from writers at CUNY Hunter College, University of Iowa, Indiana University, and Yale. It's another reminder of how the Alliance continues to be a major force in American theater, not just locally but nationally. Having this kind of new work development happening right in our backyard is pretty special.

stories.headline: Eats Reopens in West End After Closure
stories.summary: The beloved West End restaurant Eats has reopened after being closed, giving the neighborhood back one of its dining staples. It's good news for the West End's restaurant scene, which has been working to rebuild after losing several longtime establishments in recent years.
stories.body:
Great news for the West End: Eats is back. The neighborhood restaurant reopened after being closed, and honestly, the West End needs all the good dining options it can get right now.

Eats has been a fixture in the community for years, the kind of place that becomes part of the neighborhood fabric. When restaurants like this close, it's not just about losing a place to eat, it's about losing a community gathering spot where people know your name and your usual order.

The West End has been working hard to revitalize its dining scene after losing several longtime establishments over the past few years. Having Eats back open is a win for residents who don't want to have to drive across town every time they want a decent meal. It's also a sign that maybe the neighborhood's restaurant scene is starting to stabilize.

stories.headline: Decatur Commission to Discuss Replacing WatchFest Mural
stories.summary: The Decatur City Commission will discuss replacing the WatchFest mural during their Monday meeting. The mural has been a local landmark, so any changes will likely generate plenty of community discussion about what should replace it and whether it's time for something new.
stories.body:
Decatur's getting ready for what could be an interesting City Commission meeting Monday night. One of the items on the agenda is discussing replacement of the WatchFest mural, which has been part of the downtown landscape for years.

Anyone who's spent time in downtown Decatur knows this mural, it's become one of those local landmarks that people use as a reference point when giving directions. So any talk of replacing it is going to get people's attention, both from folks who think it's time for something fresh and those who want to preserve what's there.

The commission meets at 7:30 p.m. at City Hall on Monday, with dinner and executive sessions beforehand. Given how passionate Decatur residents can be about changes to their downtown, this discussion could be pretty lively. It'll be interesting to see what kind of community input they get on what should replace it, if anything.

stories.headline: Southwest Atlanta Leads City in Crime Reduction
stories.summary: Zone 4, covering neighborhoods from Cascade Heights to Princeton Lakes, saw a 23% overall drop in crime and earned the Atlanta Police Department's 2025 Crime Reduction Award. Property crimes were hit especially hard, with car break-ins down 38% and vehicle thefts down 41%. It's proof that community policing strategies can actually work.
stories.body:
Southwest Atlanta is having a moment, and it's the good kind. Zone 4, which covers everything from Cascade Heights to Princeton Lakes, just earned the Atlanta Police Department's Crime Reduction Award for 2025. They haven't held that distinction since 2020.

The numbers are pretty impressive: 23% overall drop in crime, with some categories doing even better. Car break-ins fell 38%, vehicle thefts dropped 41%, and burglaries were down 16%. Chief Darin Schierbaum credits a combination of more officers on the street, better technology, and residents actually partnering with police.

What's encouraging is hearing from residents who say they actually feel safer in their neighborhoods. That's the real test, not just the statistics but whether people feel comfortable walking their dogs at night or letting their kids play outside. Southwest Atlanta has been working hard to shake off old perceptions, and results like this show that community-police partnerships can actually make a difference.
